serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of durian coffee (approximately 300 g) is a typical single-serve portion as sold by food delivery platforms in Singapore and Vietnam.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia; USDA FoodData Central — Durian raw (FDC ID 168192) and Brewed coffee (FDC ID 171890); Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition Database — Condensed milk
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for durian coffee as a composite beverage. Nutrient values were estimated by component-based modelling: durian fruit (FAO/INFOODS SEA; USDA FDC ID 168192), brewed coffee (USDA FDC ID 171890), and condensed milk (HPB Singapore). Input record values (energy 116.67 kcal, protein 1.67 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 15.0 g, sugar 11.67 g, sodium 33.33 mg, dietary fibre 1.0 g, calcium 50.0 mg, iron 0.17 mg, cholesterol 10.0 mg, saturated fat 3.0 g, trans fat 0.17 g, vitamin C 5.0 mg per 100 g) are consistent with a blended beverage of approximately 40% durian pulp, 40% brewed coffee, and 20% condensed milk by weight. Values retained from input with source upgraded to approved references.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — Durian fruit and sweetened coffee beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for durian coffee as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous components: durian fruit GI ~49–55 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and sweetened condensed-milk coffee beverages GI ~53–60. Weighted blend of approximately 40% durian pulp and 20% condensed milk in a coffee base supports a low GI estimate of 55. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 45.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.67 g sugar per 100 g (35.0 g per 300 g serving), exceeding the AHA/WHO 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; condensed milk drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain independent of the low absolute sodium content (33.33 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its high added-sugar content from condensed milk; if consumed, request an unsweetened version prepared with black coffee and a small portion of fresh durian only, and limit serving size to no more than 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (11.67 g sugar/100 g; 35.0 g per 300 g serving) with a low GI of 55 and a high glycemic load of 24.75 per serving; condensed milk provides concentrated rapidly-absorbed sugars causing significant postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage; the combination of condensed milk, natural durian fructose, and added sugar produces a high glycemic load per serving that is incompatible with blood glucose management, and an unsweetened black coffee with a small amount of fresh durian is the only clinically acceptable modification.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.0 g/100 g (9.0 g per 300 g serving) from condensed milk, combined with 11.67 g/100 g added sugar and 10.0 mg/100 g cholesterol; high sugar intake from SSBs is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this beverage due to its saturated fat and high added-sugar content, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and triglycerides; if consumed occasionally, portion should not exceed 150 g and condensed milk should be omitted or replaced with unsweetened low-fat milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Durian Coffee (Cà Phê Sầu Riêng; 榴莲咖啡; Kopi Durian) is a Southeast Asian blended beverage that combines brewed robusta or arabica coffee with fresh or frozen durian pulp and sweetened condensed milk, producing a rich, creamy drink with the distinctive pungent-sweet aroma of durian layered over the bitter depth of coffee. The beverage originated in Vietnam as Cà Phê Sầu Riêng and has become popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, particularly through food delivery platforms. It is typically prepared by blending durian pulp with hot or iced brewed coffee and condensed milk, then serving over ice in a 300 g cup. The flavour profile is intensely sweet and creamy with a strong durian sulphurous-fruity note and a bitter coffee undertone. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 116.67 kcal, 1.67 g protein, 5.0 g fat (3.0 g saturated), 15.0 g carbohydrate, and 11.67 g sugar, making it a calorie-dense, high-sugar beverage. The high sugar content (11.67 g/100 g) classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which is clinically significant for type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic load per 300 g serving is approximately 24.75 (low-high), driven by condensed milk and natural durian fructose. Calcium (50 mg/100 g) from condensed milk and vitamin C (5 mg/100 g) from durian are notable micronutrients. The beverage is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy (condensed milk) and is not suitable for vegans or the lactose-intolerant.
common_user_queries: Is durian coffee suitable for diabetics?
Can I drink durian coffee if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for durian coffee as a composite beverage. Nutrient values were estimated by component-based modelling using FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central, and HPB Singapore data for individual components (durian, brewed coffee, condensed milk). Input record values are internally consistent with the estimated composition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for durian and sweetened coffee beverages. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are all approved primary sources and the blend ratios are consistent with standard recipe proportions.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite durian-coffee-condensed-milk beverage are not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central, or HPB Singapore as of 2026. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.17 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with trace trans fat from condensed milk; this value is retained as plausible. All core macros (protein_g 1.67, fat_g 5.0, carbohydrate_g 15.0) are non-zero and sourced from component-based estimation using approved sources. No zero-macro justification is required. The food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) per WHO/AHA criteria (sugar ≥5 g/100 g in a beverage). Country of origin is Singapore per input record; Vietnamese name Cà Phê Sầu Riêng is retained as the dish has strong Vietnamese culinary roots. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) and Vietnamese as the dish's primary cultural origin language. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement data for this specific beverage combination is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GI category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' as per University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI 55 is borderline but generally considered low).
